prompt: A small team is choosing between writing software in TypeScript or Python for a new internal data tool. Walk through how you would help them decide, in prose (not a bulleted list).
response:
I’d start by making “internal data tool” concrete. Is this primarily a web application with a polished UI that lets colleagues explore data and kick off jobs, a service that ingests, transforms, and publishes datasets on a schedule, a set of CLIs for analysts, or some combination of those? Who will extend it six months from now—backend engineers, frontend engineers, data scientists, or analysts—and where will it run (browser, server, containers, serverless, edge)? I’d also pin down the shape and scale of the data work: interactive queries over tens of thousands of rows, batch transformations of tens or hundreds of millions, or near–real-time event processing; the types of data stores involved; and whether you expect machine learning or exploratory analysis to be first‑class concerns.

With that context, I’d walk the team through how each language lines up with the realities of the tool.

If the heart of the product is a web UI and quick iteration on product features, TypeScript gives you one language across client and server, strong refactoring safety, and a mature app stack (React/Next.js, tRPC/NestJS, Prisma/Drizzle). It shines when most of the “data” work is orchestration, API composition, schema validation, and I/O, and when you want to deploy to Node, serverless functions, or edge runtimes. Real‑time UX, websockets, and streamy workflows are ergonomically simpler in Node. You can certainly do data work in TS—DuckDB has good Node bindings, Apache Arrow has a solid JS implementation, and there are data‑frame style libraries like Arquero and Polars bindings for Node—but the breadth and depth of the scientific/data ecosystem is thinner, and you’ll spend more time assembling building blocks when you move beyond moderate volumes and straightforward transforms.

If the center of gravity is data manipulation and analysis—reading and writing Parquet/Arrow, complex joins and window functions, columnar transformations over large datasets, statistics or ML, data quality checks, and scheduled pipelines—Python is usually the smoother path. Pandas and Polars make columnar work concise and fast; DuckDB, PyArrow, and the Python clients for the big warehouses are first‑class; FastAPI and Pydantic give you a pleasant way to wrap data services; and you can reach for Airflow/Prefect/DBT, Jupyter, Streamlit/Dash when you need orchestration, exploration, or lightweight internal UIs. Performance‑wise, Python leans on C/Rust under the hood (NumPy, Polars, DuckDB), so CPU‑bound work is typically excellent despite the GIL, and the ecosystem for data testing, profiling, serialization, and visualization is deeper. Packaging and deployment have improved a lot (uv/pipx/poetry, Docker) but still require some discipline; TypeScript’s packaging story via npm is a bit more uniform.

Team composition matters as much as the workload. A JS‑heavy team that needs to ship a UI quickly will move faster and make fewer mistakes with TypeScript, especially if the data layer is mostly I/O and modest transforms. A team with data scientists or analysts in the loop will be more productive in Python, where notebooks, familiar libraries, and a “batteries for data” culture reduce friction. Static typing exists on both sides now—TypeScript enforces it by default; Python’s type hints with mypy or pyright are excellent if you adopt them—but you’ll get more compile‑time safety “for free” in TS, and richer off‑the‑shelf data APIs in Python.

Operationally, think about where it needs to run. If you want logic at the edge or in the browser (e.g., local DuckDB‑WASM queries on user machines), TypeScript is the natural fit. If it will live as containers scheduled by your data platform, integrate with Airflow/Databricks/Spark, or rely on notebook‑driven workflows, Python nests better. Both are fine in AWS Lambda or containers. For long‑running, CPU‑bound compute on a single process, Python’s data engines will typically outperform hand‑rolled JS; for highly concurrent network services, Node’s async I/O is delightful, though modern Python async is close.

Given all that, I wouldn’t ask you to decide by argument alone. I’d propose a short, time‑boxed spike in each stack that implements the thinnest vertical slice of your tool: ingest a representative dataset, perform two or three real transforms including a join and a window or group‑by, persist to Parquet, expose the result via a small API, and show it in a minimal UI if that’s in scope. In each spike, wire up auth, logging, tests, and packaging just enough to be honest about day‑two concerns. Measure developer time to first result, lines of code you’re comfortable owning, runtime and memory for a dataset of realistic size, friction points (e.g., missing libraries, awkward APIs), and deployment smoothness into your actual environment. Also pay attention to how easy it feels to add an unexpected requirement, like “sample 1% of rows stratified by a key” or “validate incoming payloads and surface human‑readable errors.”

From there, the decision usually becomes clear. If your slice reveals that most value is in a polished UI with modest data shaping, and your org is already Node‑centric, TypeScript lets a small team ship faster and keep the stack cohesive. If the slice shows that data manipulation, exploration, and analysis dominate, and you foresee ML, ad hoc studies, or biggish batch jobs, Python will keep you moving with less custom infrastructure. If you truly need both—a rich web app and heavy data compute—you can split the system: a Python service that owns the data engine and a TypeScript app that owns UX and orchestration, with Arrow/Parquet or HTTP/GRPC between them. For a small team, though, I’d avoid a two‑language split unless the spike proves it pays for itself.

If you forced a default without further input, I’d bias toward Python when the “tool” is a data engine with some UI attached, and toward TypeScript when the “tool” is a productized internal app whose data work is primarily I/O and light transforms. The spike will turn that bias into evidence, and the team’s existing skills and runtime constraints should break any remaining tie.
